source_text: sources/comments/files/20251121-5493__GridStor-LLC/RM26-4 Comment of GridStor on ANOPR re ESRs and Large Loads.txt
overall_summary: GridStor, an independent power producer of transmission-scale battery storage, argues that any large-load interconnection rule must treat Energy Storage Resource (ESR) charging as distinct from large customer load so that reforms do not slow ESR deployment. It supports the ANOPR proposals to study paired loads and generation together and to expedite curtailable and dispatchable loads, asking that both extend to ESRs, and it recommends studying ESR charging and discharging together during the generator interconnection process to fill the gap between Order 841 and Order 2023.

quotes.text: GridStor submits these comments to assist the Commission as it moves forward with a rulemaking on these issues, emphasizing the need to distinguish the treatment of Energy Storage Resource (“ESR”) charging in the development of a Final Rule or other action that affects the interconnection of large loads.
quotes.concern: Core ask: distinguish ESR charging from large loads in any final rule

quotes.text: Energy Storage Resources (ESRs) are critical to helping get large loads online faster and reliably.
quotes.concern: ESRs speed up large-load interconnection and reliability

quotes.text: Batteries enable flexible demand management for data centers to ensure business continuity while complying with such requirements.
quotes.concern: Batteries provide the flexibility data centers need to meet curtailment requirements

quotes.text: In subsequent action to this ANOPR, the Commission must carefully distinguish ESR charging from large customer loads.
quotes.concern: ESR charging must be treated separately from large customer load

quotes.text: To the extent that the Commission seeks to put new requirements on large loads, it is imperative that ESRs be considered distinctly and separately from large loads.
quotes.concern: New large-load requirements should not be applied to ESRs

quotes.text: For example, it would be inappropriate and onerously duplicative to apply large loads queuing requirements as discussed in the ANOPR to apply to ESRs. Today, ESRs already participate in generator interconnection queuing processes with required deposits and readiness requirements, and ESRs execute interconnection agreements in a well-understood manner.
quotes.concern: Applying large-load queuing and deposit rules to ESRs would be duplicative since ESRs already meet generator queue requirements

quotes.text: In particular, we ask that any Commission consideration of such coordinated study include the load side of paired facilities like ESRs, not only the generation side.
quotes.concern: Coordinated study of paired facilities must include the load side (ESR charging), not just generation

quotes.text: The present approach to studying loads independently creates a competitive dynamic between any paired ESR and large loads, forcing them to vie for transmission capacity rather than provide desired mutual benefit.
quotes.concern: Studying paired loads independently makes ESR and load compete for transmission capacity

quotes.text: The Commission should enable studies of large loads and paired ESRs as coordinated loads, i.e., with integrated control that act as a single resource.
quotes.concern: Recommends studying paired large loads and ESRs as a single coordinated resource

quotes.text: To the extent that the Commission seeks to develop rules to expedite transmission service studies of large loads that are flexible and curtailable, we respectfully request that such rules also enable speedy and timely transmission service studies of ESR charging.
quotes.concern: Expedited study of curtailable loads should also cover ESR charging

quotes.text: Precedent exists in RTO/ISO tariffs for load interconnection processes that need no longer than 60-90 days to execute fully, and similar timeframes could be applied to ESR charging studies.
quotes.concern: Proposes a 60 to 90 day expedited timeframe for ESR charging studies

quotes.text: It is also worth exploring whether RTOs/ISOs might offer different forms of transmission service to support such flexible and curtailable loads.
quotes.concern: Suggests new transmission service forms (non-firm, conditional firm) for flexible loads

quotes.text: Other large loads and ESRs would benefit from a “conditional firm” transmission service, where pre-defined conditions (e.g., EEA, conservative operations, etc) require curtailment but where transmission service is otherwise expected—an improvement from as-available service that has little predictability.
quotes.concern: Proposes a conditional firm transmission service that curtails only under defined conditions

quotes.text: To that end, the Commission should move reforms to fill the gap left between Order 841 and Order 2023.
quotes.concern: Asks FERC to fill the regulatory gap between Order 841 and Order 2023 on ESR charging study

quotes.text: Therefore, we recommend that the Commission consider whether study of ESR charging should be more optimally conducted during the generator interconnection process and prior to a generator interconnection agreement, instead of in a separate load study after a generator interconnection agreement.
quotes.concern: Recommends studying ESR charging during the generator interconnection process, before the GIA

quotes.text: Such upgrades would be allocated following the established cost-allocation principles applied to other interconnection upgrades.
quotes.concern: ESR charging upgrades should follow established interconnection cost-allocation principles

bins.name: Treat ESR charging distinctly
bins.description: GridStor's central position is that ESR charging is integral to a supply resource and must be treated separately from large customer load. It warns that applying new large-load requirements to ESRs would slow storage deployment and asks the Commission to consider ESRs distinctly in any subsequent rule.

bins.name: Storage flexibility serves large loads
bins.description: GridStor argues that batteries are inherently flexible and curtailable, enabling demand management for data centers and helping large loads meet ride-through and curtailment requirements. It wants reforms for flexible loads to recognize and extend to ESRs.

bins.name: Avoid discriminatory rules against ESRs
bins.description: GridStor asks that new large-load rules not inadvertently disadvantage ESRs, calling it imperative that ESRs be considered separately and that large-load queuing requirements not be imposed on resources already in generator queues.

bins.name: No duplicative ESR queue deposits
bins.description: GridStor opposes applying large-load queuing and deposit requirements to ESRs, noting that ESRs already participate in generator interconnection queues with required deposits and readiness requirements, making added requirements onerously duplicative.

bins.name: Coordinated study of paired ESR and load
bins.description: GridStor supports the ANOPR proposal to study paired load and generation together but asks that the load side, including ESR charging, be included so paired ESRs and large loads are studied as a single coordinated resource rather than competing for transmission capacity. It recommends ESR charging be studied during the generator interconnection process before the agreement is executed.

bins.name: Integrated study of paired facilities
bins.description: GridStor wants paired large loads and ESRs studied as coordinated loads with integrated control, arguing that independent study forces them to vie for transmission capacity and delays and raises the cost of both.

bins.name: Recognize co-located load and storage
bins.description: GridStor frames ESR charging paired with large load like a co-located behind-the-meter configuration and asks the Commission to recognize this pairing when distinguishing ESR treatment and coordinating studies.

bins.name: Expedite curtailable load and ESR studies
bins.description: GridStor supports the ANOPR proposal to expedite study of curtailable and dispatchable loads and requests that expedited transmission service studies also apply to ESR charging, citing existing 60 to 90 day load interconnection timeframes as a model.

bins.name: New transmission service options
bins.description: GridStor suggests RTOs/ISOs offer new forms of transmission service for flexible loads and ESRs, including a non-firm service driven by locational marginal pricing and a conditional firm service that curtails only under predefined conditions.

bins.name: Fill the Order 841 to 2023 gap
bins.description: GridStor recommends FERC fill the gap between Order 841, which declined to require study of ESR charging in the generator interconnection process, and Order 2023, which gave directives on how to study it, by allowing ESR charging to be studied during interconnection before the generator interconnection agreement.

bins.name: Established cost allocation for ESR upgrades
bins.description: GridStor states that upgrades identified for ESR charging should be allocated under the same established cost-allocation principles applied to other interconnection upgrades.

bins.name: ESR charging upgrade cost allocation
bins.description: GridStor addresses how the costs of network upgrades needed for ESR charging should be handled, recommending they follow the established interconnection cost-allocation framework rather than a separate treatment.
